title.emphasis: Part II: A Pattern of Arbitrary and Discriminatory Enforcement

section.para: The discretionary power codified in payment processor AUPs is not a theoretical risk; it is actively and systematically wielded to enforce a specific moral ordering of commerce. An analysis of enforcement patterns reveals that these policies are not applied as neutral, risk-based tools. Instead, they are used to target and suppress specific categories of legal content and the communities that create them, particularly those related to adult content and LGBTQ+ expression. This pattern of discriminatory enforcement provides the factual basis for legal challenges under California's robust civil rights laws.

title.emphasis: 2.1 The Moral Ordering of "High-Risk" Commerce: Gambling vs. Adult Content

section.para: The pretextual nature of the processors' "risk management" justification is most clearly revealed by contrasting their treatment of two different "high-risk" industries: online gambling and adult content. Both industries are associated with elevated risks of chargebacks, fraud, and legal complexities.28 However, their treatment by major processors is starkly different.

PayPal's AUP, for example, explicitly allows for transactions related to online gambling, sports betting, and games of skill, provided the merchant obtains "pre-approval" and operates in a jurisdiction where the activity is legal.14 Similarly, Stripe's policies contemplate the possibility of supporting gambling-related businesses, subject to review.5 This demonstrates a capacity for nuanced, risk-based underwriting, where the processor can assess a merchant's specific business model and legal compliance.

In stark contrast, these same processors often impose blanket prohibitions on legal adult content. Stripe's policy, for instance, broadly bans "pornography and other sexually explicit materials (including literature, imagery, and other media)".5 This is not a risk-based assessment but a content-based moral judgment. The differential treatment cannot be explained by risk alone; it reveals an underlying corporate value system—a "moral ordering of sexuality"—where one form of "vice" is deemed a manageable business risk while another is prohibited on moral grounds.24 This inconsistency undermines the claim that these AUPs are legitimate, non-discriminatory business tools and instead exposes them as instruments for enforcing a particular corporate morality.

title.emphasis: 2.2 Targeting the Margins: The De-platforming of Sex Workers and LGBTQ+ Creators

section.para: The real-world impact of this moral ordering falls disproportionately on marginalized communities. Extensive research documents a pattern of "financial discrimination" where sex workers and creators in the legal adult industry are systematically de-platformed and denied access to basic financial services essential for their livelihood and safety.19

This discriminatory enforcement extends to LGBTQ+ creators, whose work is often miscategorized as "sexually explicit" or "obscene" even when it is not. In several high-profile cases, pressure from payment processors has led platforms like Steam and Itch.io to de-index or remove not just the specific content targeted by activist campaigns, but a wide swath of adult-themed and LGBTQ+ games, books, and comics.6 In some instances, creators have been de-platformed for entirely non-explicit LGBTQ+ content due to erroneous interpretations of vague AUPs by processor support staff, highlighting the inherent danger of such policies.37 This pattern of over-enforcement and collateral censorship demonstrates that the primary harm of these policies is borne by creators whose expression falls outside of a narrow, normative mainstream.
The following table provides a sample of documented cases of de-platforming and financial censorship targeting creators of adult and LGBTQ+ content.

row.entry: Widespread de-indexing and removal of NSFW games following pressure from an anti-porn lobby group.
Patreon account terminated due to alleged off-platform violations of Visa/Mastercard morality standards.
Systemic account closures and denial of services, often with vague or no explanation.
Removal of queer romance and trans-authored books as part of a broader adult content purge.
Stripe support staff wrongly informed users that all LGBTQ+ content, explicit or not, was prohibited. Stripe later apologized.
Account terminated and funds seized with no specific reason given, isolating the creator from the freelance economy.

section.para: This enforcement regime creates a discriminatory feedback loop. By de-platforming creators from marginalized communities, processors force them into more precarious financial channels or offline work, which increases their real-world vulnerability.26 This heightened vulnerability can then be cited as evidence of their "high-risk" nature, justifying further financial exclusion. The policy, therefore, manufactures the very conditions it purports to be protecting against, trapping these communities in a cycle of systemic marginalization.
